title: Find pair with given sum
description: Find two elements in a sorted array whose sum equals a target value

examples.explanation: Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1; j=1, arr[1]=2, sum=3 (not 7); j=2, arr[2]=3, sum=4; j=3, arr[3]=4, sum=5; j=4, arr[4]=5, sum=6. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=2; j=2, arr[2]=3, sum=5; j=3, arr[3]=4, sum=6; j=4, arr[4]=5, sum=7 (match), return [2, 5].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Check all possible pairs in the array by using nested loops to see if any pair sums to the target. Use this only for small arrays or as a baseline, as it is inefficient for larger inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Pair Sum in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Find two elements in a sorted array whose sum equals a target value using nested loops.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the pair sum problem by systematically checking every possible pair of elements in the array. For a sorted array of size n, we use two nested loops: the outer loop iterates through each element as the first candidate, and the inner loop iterates through all subsequent elements as the second candidate. For each pair (arr[i], arr[j]), we compute their sum and compare it to the target. If a match is found, we return the pair or their indices; otherwise, we continue until all pairs are exhausted. This method is straightforward but inefficient, with O(n²) time complexity due to the nested loops, and O(1) space complexity as it uses only a few variables. It serves as a baseline for understanding more optimized approaches like two-pointer or hash-based methods. The intuition is that by checking all combinations, we guarantee finding a solution if it exists, but this comes at the cost of high computational overhead for large arrays. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds (e.g., inner loop starting from i instead of i+1, leading to duplicate checks or self-pairs), not handling edge cases like empty arrays or no solution, and inefficient early termination. In pseudocode: initialize n = len(arr); for i from 0 to n-2: for j from i+1 to n-1: if arr[i] + arr[j] == target: return [arr[i], arr[j]]; return None. This connects to L1 by relying on the key subroutine of pair checking within loops.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1; j=1, arr[1]=2, sum=3 (not 7); j=2, arr[2]=3, sum=4; j=3, arr[3]=4, sum=5; j=4, arr[4]=5, sum=6. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=2; j=2, arr[2]=3, sum=5; j=3, arr[3]=4, sum=6; j=4, arr[4]=5, sum=7 (match), return [2, 5].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Nested Loop Pair Checking
ladders.desc: Implement nested loops to iterate through all possible pairs in an array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the core of the brute force approach by using two nested loops to generate and check every unique pair of elements in the array. The outer loop selects the first element (index i), and the inner loop selects the second element (index j) from i+1 onward, ensuring each pair is considered exactly once without repetition. For each pair (arr[i], arr[j]), we compute the sum and compare it to a target, returning the pair if a match is found. The intuition is that by systematically covering all combinations, we exhaustively search for a solution. The time complexity is O(n²) because for n elements, there are n*(n-1)/2 pairs, leading to quadratic growth in operations. Space complexity is O(1) as only loop indices and sum variables are used. This feeds into L0 by providing the mechanism to solve the full pair sum problem. Common mistakes include starting the inner loop from 0 (causing duplicate checks) or i (checking self-pairs, which are invalid for distinct elements), and not handling cases where no pair exists. In pseudocode: for i in range(0, n-1): for j in range(i+1, n): if arr[i] + arr[j] == target: return [arr[i], arr[j]]; return None. This builds on L2's core logic of pair generation.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1; j=1, arr[1]=2, sum=3; j=2, arr[2]=3, sum=4. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=2; j=2, arr[2]=3, sum=5 (match), return [2, 3].
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=4; j=1, arr[1]=5, sum=9; j=2, arr[2]=6, sum=10. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=5; j=2, arr[2]=6, sum=11 (match), return [5, 6].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generating All Unique Pairs
ladders.desc: Generate all unique pairs of indices in an array without repetition.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on the technique of generating every unique pair of indices (i, j) from an array of size n, where i < j to avoid duplicates and self-pairs. The method uses combinatorial reasoning: for n elements, there are C(n,2) = n*(n-1)/2 possible pairs. We achieve this by iterating i from 0 to n-2 and j from i+1 to n-1, ensuring each pair is considered once. The intuition is that by covering all combinations systematically, we can apply any operation (like sum checking) to find a solution. This is fundamental to brute force algorithms and connects to problems like finding pairs, triplets, or subsets. Time complexity is O(n²) due to the nested iteration over pairs, and space complexity is O(1). It feeds into L1 by providing the pair generation framework for the nested loop sub-routine. Common mistakes include generating pairs with i >= j (leading to duplicates or invalid checks) or missing pairs by incorrect loop bounds. In pseudocode: pairs = []; for i in range(0, n-1): for j in range(i+1, n): pairs.append((i, j)); return pairs. This builds on L3's concept of array iteration.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Array with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Use nested loops to access elements in an array multiple times.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using two nested loops to traverse an array, allowing access to each element multiple times for comparisons or operations. The outer loop iterates through each element as a reference point, and the inner loop iterates through the array again, typically starting from a different index to avoid redundant checks. The intuition is that nested loops enable pairwise or multi-element interactions, which are essential for algorithms requiring comparisons between all elements. Time complexity is O(n²) if both loops run n times, but it can vary based on loop bounds. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to generate pairs through specific loop configurations. Common mistakes include infinite loops due to incorrect increment logic, off-by-one errors in loop ranges, and inefficient nesting that leads to unnecessary operations. In pseudocode: for i in range(0, n): for j in range(0, n): # operation with arr[i] and arr[j]. This builds on L4's basic operation of array access.

testCases.input: arr = [0, 1], operation: check if arr[i] == arr[j]
arr = [100], operation: access arr[i] and arr[j]
arr = [1, 3, 5], operation: store pairs in list

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve or modify elements in an array using their indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing elements in an array by their index positions, which is fundamental to array manipulation in programming. An array is a contiguous block of memory storing elements, and each element has a unique index starting from 0. To access an element, we use the syntax arr[index], which retrieves the value at that position in constant time O(1). The intuition is that arrays provide direct access via indices, enabling efficient data retrieval and updates. This operation is crucial for loops and algorithms that process array data. Time complexity is O(1) per access, and space complexity is O(1) if only reading. It feeds into L3 by allowing nested loops to read array elements for comparisons. Common mistakes include accessing indices out of bounds (e.g., index >= len(arr)), which causes errors, or using 1-based indexing by mistake. In pseudocode: element = arr[i] # access; arr[i] = value # modification. This builds on L5's concept of arrays as data structures.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array indices: 0->10, 1->20, 2->30. Step 2: Access arr[1] returns value 20.
Step 1: Access arr[0] -> 5. Step 2: Access arr[2] -> 7. Result: [5, 7].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is and how to use indices to refer to elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces arrays as a basic data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations. Each element in an array has a unique index, starting from 0 for the first element, 1 for the second, and so on. Arrays allow efficient access to elements by their position, making them ideal for storing lists of items like numbers or strings. The intuition is that arrays organize data in a linear sequence, enabling systematic processing through loops. Key properties include fixed size (in many languages), homogeneous data types, and constant-time access via indices. This is a prerequisite for any algorithm involving data traversal, such as the brute force pair sum problem. Time complexity for access is O(1), and space complexity depends on the number of elements. It feeds into L4 by providing the basis for element access operations. Common mistakes include confusing indices with values (e.g., thinking index 1 means the first element) or assuming arrays start at index 1. In pseudocode: arr = [value1, value2, ...] # declaration; first_element = arr[0]. This is the simplest concept needed for beginners.

examples.trace: Step 1: The array has 3 elements. Step 2: Index 0 holds 5, index 1 holds 10, index 2 holds 15.
Step 1: List indices: 0->1, 1->2, 2->3, 3->4. Step 2: Access index 2 returns value 3.

approaches.name: Two Pointer O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers starting at the beginning and end of the sorted array, moving them inward based on whether the current sum is less than or greater than the target. This efficiently finds the pair in linear time by exploiting the sorted order.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Two Pointer Solution for Pair Sum
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the two-pointer algorithm to find a pair summing to target in a sorted array.
ladders.explanation: The two-pointer algorithm efficiently solves the pair sum problem in O(n) time by exploiting the sorted nature of the array. The intuition is that with a sorted array, we can intelligently move pointers from both ends toward the center, eliminating large portions of the search space with each comparison. We initialize two pointers: left at index 0 (smallest element) and right at index n-1 (largest element). At each step, we calculate the sum of elements at these pointers. If the sum equals the target, we've found our pair. If the sum is less than the target, we need a larger sum, so we move the left pointer rightward (increasing the smaller element). If the sum is greater than the target, we need a smaller sum, so we move the right pointer leftward (decreasing the larger element). This process continues until we find a pair or the pointers cross (indicating no solution). The algorithm runs in O(n) time since each pointer moves at most n times, and uses O(1) space as we only store two pointers. This approach is optimal for sorted arrays because it avoids the O(n²) brute-force checking while maintaining simplicity. Common mistakes include not handling empty arrays, forgetting to check pointer bounds, or incorrectly moving pointers when duplicates exist. The connection to L1 is that this level implements the complete solution using the key comparison and pointer movement logic.

examples.trace:
Step 1: left=0 (value=1), right=4 (value=6), sum=7 > target=6 → move right leftward
Step 2: left=0 (1), right=3 (4), sum=5 < target=6 → move left rightward
Step 3: left=1 (2), right=3 (4), sum=6 == target → found pair at indices 1 and 3

Step 1: left=0 (2), right=3 (11), sum=13 > target=11 → move right leftward
Step 2: left=0 (2), right=2 (9), sum=11 == target → found pair at indices 0 and 2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pointer Movement Based on Sum Comparison
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that compares current sum with target and moves pointers accordingly.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine encapsulates the decision logic that drives the two-pointer algorithm. The intuition is that in a sorted array, comparing the current sum with the target tells us exactly which pointer to move and why. When sum < target, we know that increasing the left pointer (which points to a smaller element) will increase our sum, moving us closer to the target. When sum > target, decreasing the right pointer (which points to a larger element) will decrease our sum. This systematic adjustment is what gives the algorithm its O(n) efficiency—each comparison eliminates multiple potential pairs from consideration. The algorithm maintains a while loop that continues while left < right. Inside, it calculates current_sum = arr[left] + arr[right], then branches: if equal to target, return the indices; if less than target, increment left; if greater than target, decrement right. If the loop exits without finding a pair, return [-1, -1]. Time complexity is O(n) as each pointer moves at most n times, and space is O(1). This connects to L0 as it's the engine of the full solution, and to L2 as it relies on the insight that sorted order enables predictable pointer movement. Common mistakes include using <= instead of < in the loop condition (which could cause errors with single elements), or moving the wrong pointer for the comparison.

examples.trace:
Step 1: sum=1+5=6 == target → return [0,3]
If we continued: sum=6 → found immediately

Step 1: sum=1+6=7 == target → return [0,3]
If target was 8: Step1: sum=7<8 → left=1; Step2: sum=3+6=9>8 → right=2; Step3: sum=3+4=7<8 → left=2; loop ends

ladders.title: Core Logic — Exploiting Sorted Order for Efficient Search
ladders.desc: Understand how sorted array property enables the two-pointer approach to find pairs efficiently.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that a sorted array allows us to make intelligent decisions about which elements to consider next, reducing the search space dramatically. In an unsorted array, finding a pair that sums to a target typically requires checking all possible pairs (O(n²)). However, when the array is sorted, we can use the ordering to our advantage. The intuition is that if we start with the smallest and largest elements (pointers at ends), their sum gives us boundary information: if it's too small, we know that moving the left pointer rightward (to a larger element) will increase the sum; if it's too large, moving the right pointer leftward (to a smaller element) will decrease the sum. This creates a controlled search that narrows in on the solution. Mathematically, for any left pointer position i and right pointer position j, all pairs (i,k) with k>j have sums >= arr[i]+arr[j] due to sorting, and all pairs (k,j) with k<i have sums <= arr[i]+arr[j]. This monotonic property guarantees that our pointer movements never skip over a valid solution. The algorithm effectively performs a linear scan from both ends, making at most 2n movements total. Time is O(n), space O(1). This connects to L1 as it provides the theoretical foundation for the pointer movement logic, and to L3 as it builds upon the concept of array traversal. Common mistakes include not recognizing that this only works for sorted arrays, or trying to apply it to unsorted data.

examples.output: Using sorted property to find pair
examples.trace: Initial: left=0 (1), right=3 (7), sum=8 == target → found immediately. If target=10: Step1: sum=8<10 → left=1 (3); Step2: sum=3+7=10 == target → found. The sorting ensures moving left increases sum predictably.
Step1: left=0 (2), right=3 (8), sum=10<14 → left=1 (4); Step2: sum=4+8=12<14 → left=2 (6); Step3: sum=6+8=14 == target → found. Only 3 comparisons vs 6 in brute force.

ladders.title: Building Block — Traversing Array with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Practice moving two indices through an array from opposite ends until they meet.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the mechanical skill of controlling two pointers that start at opposite ends of an array and move toward each other. The intuition is that many array problems can be solved efficiently by processing elements from both ends simultaneously, rather than just from one end. We initialize left=0 and right=n-1. In a loop while left < right, we can perform operations, then typically move left rightward (left++) and/or right leftward (right--). The loop condition left < right ensures the pointers don't cross, which is important for avoiding redundant checks or errors. This pattern is fundamental because it allows O(n) processing with minimal space. The step-by-step algorithm is: 1. Check if left < right; if not, exit. 2. Perform desired operation with arr[left] and arr[right]. 3. Decide how to move pointers based on the problem logic (in this building block, we simply move both inward: left++, right--). 4. Repeat. Time complexity is O(n) since each pointer moves at most n/2 times, and space is O(1). This connects to L2 as it provides the traversal framework needed to implement the sorted search logic, and to L4 as it uses basic pointer increments/decrements. Common mistakes include using <= instead of < (which could process an element twice), or not updating pointers correctly inside the loop.

examples.input: arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], traverse from ends to middle
examples.trace:
Step1: left=0, right=4 → process (1,5)
Step2: left=1, right=3 → process (2,4)
Step3: left=2, right=2 → loop ends (left not < right)

Step1: left=0, right=5 → (10,60)
Step2: left=1, right=4 → (20,50)
Step3: left=2, right=3 → (30,40)
Step4: left=3, right=2 → loop ends

testCases.input: arr = [a, b, c, d, e], traverse

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Summing Array Elements
ladders.desc: Practice accessing array elements by index and performing arithmetic comparisons.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves retrieving elements from an array using indices and performing basic arithmetic operations like addition and comparison. The intuition is that arrays provide O(1) access to any element if you know its index, making them ideal for algorithms that need to frequently compare different positions. To get an element at index i, we use arr[i]. To sum two elements at indices i and j, we compute arr[i] + arr[j]. To compare this sum to a target value, we use relational operators: ==, <, >. This is the atomic action that drives the two-pointer algorithm. The step-by-step process is: 1. Identify indices i and j. 2. Access arr[i] and arr[j]. 3. Compute sum = arr[i] + arr[j]. 4. Compare sum to target using if-else logic. Time complexity is O(1) per operation, and space is O(1) for the variables. This connects to L3 as it's the operation performed during each step of pointer traversal, and to L5 as it builds on basic array indexing. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors (e.g., using indices beyond array length), incorrect operator precedence, or type mismatches in comparisons.

examples.trace:
Step1: access arr[0]=5, arr[2]=15
Step2: sum=5+15=20
Step3: compare 20 == 20 → true

Step1: access arr[1]=4, arr[2]=7
Step2: sum=4+7=11
Step3: compare 11 > 10 → true

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Basic Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Learn how to access elements in an array and perform simple addition and comparison.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers the basics of working with arrays: understanding that an array is an ordered collection of elements where each element has an index starting from 0. To access an element, you use its index with bracket notation, e.g., arr[0] gets the first element. Arithmetic operations like addition can be performed on these elements if they are numbers. Comparisons between numbers use standard operators: == for equality, < for less than, > for greater than. The intuition is that arrays allow us to store multiple values and retrieve them quickly by position, which is essential for algorithms that need to examine different parts of the data. For example, if arr = [5, 10, 15], then arr[0] is 5, arr[1] is 10, and arr[2] is 15. Adding arr[0] + arr[1] gives 15. Comparing this sum to a target like 20 would show 15 < 20. This is the simplest building block for the pair sum problem. Step-by-step: 1. Define an array with values. 2. Choose indices to access. 3. Retrieve values. 4. Add them. 5. Compare to target. Time and space are O(1) for these operations. This connects to L4 as it provides the skills needed to perform the basic operations in the algorithm. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (thinking index 1 is first element), confusing array values with indices, or incorrect operator usage.

examples.trace:
Step1: arr[0] = 3
Step2: arr[2] = 11
Step3: sum = 3 + 11 = 14
Step4: compare 14 == 14 → true

Step1: arr[1] = 4
Step2: arr[1] = 4 (same index)
Step3: sum = 4 + 4 = 8
Step4: compare 8 == 8 → true

approaches.name: HashMap O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the array, storing each element in a hash map and checking if the complement (target minus current element) exists in the map. This works for unsorted arrays but uses extra space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Pair with Given Sum Using HashMap
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find two elements in a sorted array whose sum equals a target value using HashMap approach.
ladders.explanation: The HashMap approach solves the two-sum problem efficiently for both sorted and unsorted arrays by leveraging constant-time lookups. The intuition is that for each element `arr[i]`, we need to check if its complement `target - arr[i]` exists among previously seen elements. By storing each element in a hash map as we iterate, we can instantly check if the complement has been encountered. This transforms the problem from a nested loop O(n²) search to a single pass O(n) solution. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty hash map. 2) Iterate through each element in the array. 3) For each element, calculate complement = target - current element. 4) Check if complement exists in the hash map. If yes, return the pair (complement, current element). 5) Otherwise, add current element to the hash map with its index as value. 6) If loop completes without finding a pair, return null or appropriate indicator. Time complexity is O(n) since we traverse the array once with O(1) map operations. Space complexity is O(n) for storing up to n elements in the map. Connection to L1: This full solution directly uses the key subroutine of checking complement existence in the map. Common mistakes include not handling duplicate elements correctly (the map stores indices, so duplicates overwrite, but that's fine since we check complement before adding), forgetting to check for null/empty arrays, and off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern: def find_pair(arr, target): seen = {}; for i, num in enumerate(arr): complement = target - num; if complement in seen: return [seen[complement], i]; seen[num] = i; return None.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize seen = {}. Step 2: i=0, num=1, complement=9-1=8, 8 not in seen, add 1: seen={1:0}. Step 3: i=1, num=2, complement=9-2=7, 7 not in seen, add 2: seen={1:0, 2:1}. Step 4: i=2, num=3, complement=9-3=6, 6 not in seen, add 3: seen={1:0, 2:1, 3:2}. Step 5: i=3, num=4, complement=9-4=5, 5 not in seen, add 4: seen={1:0, 2:1, 3:2, 4:3}. Step 6: i=4, num=5, complement=9-5=4, 4 is in seen with index 3, return [3, 4].

Step 1: seen = {}. Step 2: i=0, num=10, complement=60-10=50, 50 not in seen, add 10: seen={10:0}. Step 3: i=1, num=20, complement=60-20=40, 40 not in seen, add 20: seen={10:0, 20:1}. Step 4: i=2, num=30, complement=60-30=30, 30 not in seen, add 30: seen={10:0, 20:1, 30:2}. Step 5: Loop ends, no pair found, return None.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check Complement Existence in HashMap
ladders.desc: The core function that checks if a complement exists in a hash map during iteration.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the HashMap approach, enabling the O(n) solution. The intuition is to maintain a dynamic record of elements seen so far, allowing instant verification of whether the needed partner for the current element exists. For each element in the array, we compute its complement relative to the target. By checking the hash map—a data structure with average O(1) lookup—we determine if this complement has been encountered earlier in the iteration. If found, we have a valid pair; if not, we add the current element to the map for future checks. The algorithm: 1) Input: current element, target, hash map. 2) Compute complement = target - current element. 3) Check if complement is a key in the hash map. 4) If yes, return the index of complement and current index. 5) If no, add current element to map with its index. Time complexity is O(1) per element due to hash map operations. Space complexity is O(1) extra per call, but overall O(n) for the map. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called in each iteration of the full problem's loop. Connection to L2: It relies on the core logic of using complement to find pairs. Common mistakes include not updating the map after checking (must add current element), using the wrong key-value pairing (store element as key, index as value), and mishandling when complement equals current element (works fine if complement was added earlier). Real code pattern: def check_complement(num, idx, target, seen): complement = target - num; if complement in seen: return [seen[complement], idx]; seen[num] = idx; return None.

examples.trace: Step 1: complement = 9 - 4 = 5. Step 2: Check if 5 is in seen keys {1,2,3}: 5 not present. Step 3: Add num=4 to seen with idx=3: seen becomes {1:0, 2:1, 3:2, 4:3}. Step 4: Return None.
Step 1: complement = 9 - 5 = 4. Step 2: Check if 4 is in seen keys {1,2,3,4}: 4 is present with value 3. Step 3: Return [3, 4] (indices of complement and current).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Complement Concept for Pair Sum
ladders.desc: The fundamental idea of using complement (target minus element) to find matching pairs.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the mathematical insight that drives the HashMap approach. The intuition is that for any element `a` in the array, if there exists another element `b` such that `a + b = target`, then `b` must equal `target - a`. We call `target - a` the complement of `a`. Instead of searching for `b` directly through comparisons, we can compute the complement and check for its existence. This transforms the problem from searching for pairs that sum to target into searching for individual complements. In the HashMap method, we store elements as we go, so checking for a complement becomes an O(1) operation. The algorithm: 1) For a given element and target, compute complement = target - element. 2) Determine if complement exists in the data set (e.g., array or map). 3) If exists, pair is (element, complement). This logic works regardless of array order and handles duplicates if indices are tracked. Time complexity depends on how we check existence: O(n) for linear search in array, O(1) with hash map. Space is O(1) for the computation itself. Connection to L1: This logic is applied in the subroutine to check complement in the map. Connection to L3: It builds on the concept of arithmetic operations. Common mistakes include miscalculating complement (e.g., using element - target), not considering negative numbers (works fine as complement calculation handles signs), and assuming array must be sorted (not required for this logic). Real code pattern: def get_complement(num, target): return target - num.

examples.trace: Step 1: Apply formula complement = target - element. Step 2: complement = 9 - 4 = 5. Step 3: This means if 4 is in the array, we need 5 to make sum 9.
Step 1: complement = target - element. Step 2: complement = 7 - (-3) = 7 + 3 = 10. Step 3: For -3 to pair for sum 7, we need 10.

ladders.title: Building Block — HashMap Lookup and Insertion
ladders.desc: Using a hash map to store and retrieve elements with constant-time operations.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the fundamental operations of a hash map (or dictionary) that enable efficient data management. The intuition is that a hash map allows us to store key-value pairs where keys are unique, and we can quickly check for key existence or retrieve values. In the two-sum problem, we use the array elements as keys and their indices as values. This provides O(1) average-time complexity for insertions and lookups, which is crucial for achieving overall O(n) performance. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty hash map. 2) To insert: map[key] = value, where key is the element and value is its index. If key already exists, it's overwritten (acceptable here as we only need one index per element). 3) To lookup: check if key in map, which returns a boolean, or retrieve value via map.get(key). Time complexity is O(1) per operation amortized, assuming a good hash function and load factor. Space complexity is O(n) for storing n entries. Connection to L2: This data structure is used to check complement existence efficiently. Connection to L4: It relies on basic operations like key assignment and membership testing. Common mistakes include using the wrong key-value pair (should be element:index), not handling collisions (handled internally by the map), and assuming O(1) worst-case time (it's average-case). Real code pattern: seen = {}; seen[5] = 0; if 3 in seen: idx = seen[3].

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize seen = {}. Step 2: Insert key=10, value=0: seen[10] = 0 → seen={10:0}. Step 3: Insert key=20, value=1: seen[20] = 1 → seen={10:0, 20:1}. Step 4: Insert key=30, value=2: seen[30] = 2 → seen={10:0, 20:1, 30:2}.
Step 1: Check membership: '20 in seen' evaluates to True since 20 is a key. Step 2: Retrieve value: seen[20] returns 1.

testCases.input: Insert: key=5, value=0 into empty map
Insert: key=-5, value=1 into empty map
Insert duplicate key=8, value=3 into map={8:2}

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Iteration with Index
ladders.desc: Looping through an array while tracking the index of each element.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves traversing each element in an array sequentially, which is essential for processing all elements in algorithms. The intuition is that to examine or manipulate every item in a collection, we need a systematic way to access them one by one. In the two-sum problem, we iterate through the array to consider each element as a potential part of a pair. By also tracking the index, we can record positions for output or mapping. The algorithm: 1) Start at the first element (index 0). 2) For each element, perform operations (e.g., compute complement). 3) Move to the next element until the end of the array. In Python-style, this is done with a for loop and enumerate() to get both index and value. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing n elements. Space complexity is O(1) extra if not storing data. Connection to L3: Iteration is used to feed elements into the hash map operations. Connection to L5: It builds on the concept of accessing array elements. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., stopping early), modifying the array during iteration (can cause issues), and not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: for i, num in enumerate(arr): # i is index, num is value.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize loop with i=0, num=arr[0]=10. Step 2: Process (0,10). Step 3: Increment i to 1, num=arr[1]=20. Step 4: Process (1,20). Step 5: Increment i to 2, num=arr[2]=30. Step 6: Process (2,30). Step 7: End of array, loop stops.
Step 1: Initialize loop with i=0, num=arr[0]=5. Step 2: Process (0,5). Step 3: End of array, loop stops.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Access and Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Basic operations: accessing elements in an array and performing simple arithmetic.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the simplest prerequisites needed for the two-sum problem: how to retrieve elements from an array and do basic math. The intuition is that arrays store elements in ordered positions, and we can access them using indices starting from 0. Arithmetic operations like addition and subtraction are used to compute sums and complements. In the context of two-sum, we need to access array elements to check their values and perform target minus element to find complements. The algorithm: 1) Access an element: given array arr and index i, use arr[i] to get the value. 2) Perform arithmetic: e.g., compute sum = a + b or complement = target - a. These operations are fundamental building blocks for more complex algorithms. Time complexity is O(1) for access and arithmetic. Space complexity is O(1) extra. Connection to L4: These operations are used within array iteration. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (should be 0-based), accessing out-of-bounds indices, and incorrect arithmetic order. Real code pattern: value = arr[0]; result = target - value.

examples.trace: Step 1: Access arr[1]: arr[1] = 10. Step 2: Compute complement = target - value = 20 - 10 = 10.
Step 1: Access arr[0]: arr[0] = 3. Step 2: Compute complement = 7 - 3 = 4.

approaches.name: Binary Search per Element O(n log n)
approaches.summary: For each element in the array, use binary search on the remaining sorted portion to find its complement that sums to the target. This is less efficient than two-pointer but demonstrates a divide-and-conquer approach.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Pair with Given Sum Using Binary Search per Element
ladders.desc: Complete solution: For each element, binary search the remaining sorted array for its complement.
ladders.explanation: This approach leverages the sorted nature of the array to efficiently find pairs summing to a target. The intuition is that for each element arr[i], we need to find if its complement (target - arr[i]) exists in the remaining portion of the array (i+1 to n-1). Since the array is sorted, we can use binary search on this subarray, which is O(log n) per element, leading to O(n log n) overall. The algorithm iterates through each element, and for each, performs binary search on the right subarray. If found, return the pair; if not, continue. This is less efficient than the two-pointer O(n) method but demonstrates divide-and-conquer principles. Step-by-step: 1. Loop i from 0 to n-2. 2. For each i, compute complement = target - arr[i]. 3. Perform binary search on subarray arr[i+1:n-1] for complement. 4. If found, return [arr[i], complement]. 5. If loop ends, return empty list or indicate no pair. Time: O(n log n) from n iterations with log n binary search each. Space: O(1) excluding input storage. Connection to L1: This uses the binary search subroutine from L1 as a helper. Common mistakes include not handling duplicates correctly (should find any valid pair), off-by-one errors in binary search bounds, and missing the case where complement equals the current element (allowed if distinct indices). Real code pattern: def find_pair(arr, target): n = len(arr); for i in range(n-1): comp = target - arr[i]; left, right = i+1, n-1; while left <= right: mid = (left+right)//2; if arr[mid] == comp: return [arr[i], arr[mid]]; elif arr[mid] < comp: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1; return [].

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1, complement=6. Binary search on [2,3,4,5]: left=1, right=4, mid=2 (arr[2]=3) <6, left=3; mid=3 (arr[3]=4) <6, left=4; mid=4 (arr[4]=5) <6, left=5 > right, not found. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=2, complement=5. Binary search on [3,4,5]: left=2, right=4, mid=3 (arr[3]=4) <5, left=4; mid=4 (arr[4]=5) ==5, found. Return [2,5].

Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=10, complement=50. Binary search on [20,30,40]: left=1, right=3, mid=2 (arr[2]=30) <50, left=3; mid=3 (arr[3]=40) <50, left=4 > right, not found. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=20, complement=40. Binary search on [30,40]: left=2, right=3, mid=2 (arr[2]=30) <40, left=3; mid=3 (arr[3]=40) ==40, found. Return [20,40].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Implement binary search to find a target value in a sorted subarray.
ladders.explanation: Binary search is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that efficiently locates a target in a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search interval. The intuition is that in a sorted array, comparing the middle element to the target tells us which half to search next, eliminating half the possibilities each step. This yields O(log n) time. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize left and right pointers to the start and end of the subarray. 2. While left <= right: a. Compute mid = (left + right) // 2. b. If arr[mid] == target, return mid (index found). c. If arr[mid] < target, set left = mid + 1 to search right half. d. If arr[mid] > target, set right = mid - 1 to search left half. 3. If loop exits, return -1 (not found). Time: O(log n) where n is subarray size. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each element in L0 to find complements. Common mistakes include infinite loops from incorrect termination conditions (use <= not <), integer overflow in mid calculation (use left + (right-left)//2), and not updating bounds correctly. Real code pattern: def binary_search(arr, left, right, target): while left <= right: mid = (left+right)//2; if arr[mid] == target: return mid; elif arr[mid] < target: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1; return -1.

examples.trace: Step 1: left=0, right=4, mid=2, arr[2]=6 == target, return 2.
Step 1: left=1, right=3, mid=2, arr[2]=5 >4, right=1. Step 2: left=1, right=1, mid=1, arr[1]=3 <4, left=2 > right, loop ends, return -1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Complement Search in Sorted Data
ladders.desc: For a given element, find if its complement exists in a sorted subarray.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core insight: to find a pair summing to a target in a sorted array, for each element, we can search for its complement (target - element) in the remaining sorted portion. The intuition is that if the array is sorted, we can efficiently locate complements using search algorithms like binary search, rather than checking all pairs. This reduces the problem from O(n²) to O(n log n). Step-by-step: Given array arr, target, and current index i: 1. Compute complement = target - arr[i]. 2. Determine search space: subarray from i+1 to n-1 (to ensure distinct elements). 3. Use an efficient search method (e.g., binary search) to find complement in that subarray. 4. If found, we have a valid pair; if not, move to next element. Time: O(log n) for the search per element. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This uses binary search (L1) as the search method. Common mistakes include searching the entire array instead of the subarray (which could lead to using the same element twice), not handling cases where complement equals the current element (allowed if indices differ), and incorrect complement calculation. Real code pattern: def find_complement(arr, i, target): comp = target - arr[i]; left, right = i+1, len(arr)-1; while left <= right: mid = (left+right)//2; if arr[mid] == comp: return mid; elif arr[mid] < comp: left = mid+1; else: right = mid-1; return -1.

examples.trace: Step 1: arr[1]=2, complement=5. Search subarray [3,4,5]: left=2, right=4, mid=3 (arr[3]=4) <5, left=4; mid=4 (arr[4]=5) ==5, return index 4.
Step 1: arr[0]=10, complement=40. Search subarray [20,30]: left=1, right=2, mid=1 (arr[1]=20) <40, left=2; mid=2 (arr[2]=30) <40, left=3 > right, return -1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating with Index Exclusion
ladders.desc: Loop through array elements while excluding current index from search space.
ladders.explanation: This level addresses how to traverse an array and, for each element, consider only the remaining elements for further operations. The intuition is that in pair-finding problems, we need to avoid using the same element twice, so when processing element at index i, we restrict our search or comparison to indices i+1 onwards. This ensures distinct elements in the pair. Step-by-step: 1. Loop through the array from index 0 to n-2 (since last element has no remaining elements). 2. For each i, define the subarray as arr[i+1] to arr[n-1]. 3. Perform any operation (e.g., search) on this subarray. 4. This iteration pattern is common in algorithms like two-sum or pair searches. Time: O(n) for the loop. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This iteration provides the current element and subarray for complement search in L2. Common mistakes include looping to n-1 (causing out-of-bounds for subarray), not adjusting subarray bounds correctly, and using the same element twice by including i in the search. Real code pattern: n = len(arr); for i in range(n-1): current = arr[i]; subarray = arr[i+1:n]; # operations on subarray.

examples.output: Iterations: i=0, current=1, subarray=[2,3,4]; i=1, current=2, subarray=[3,4]; i=2, current=3, subarray=[4]
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, current=arr[0]=1, subarray indices 1 to 3: [2,3,4]. Step 2: i=1, current=arr[1]=2, subarray indices 2 to 3: [3,4]. Step 3: i=2, current=arr[2]=3, subarray indices 3 to 3: [4]. Loop ends as i ranges 0 to 2.
Step 1: i=0, current=arr[0]=10, subarray indices 1 to 1: [20]. Loop ends as n=2, i ranges 0 to 0.

testCases.expected: Iterations: i=0, current=3, subarray=[6,9]; i=1, current=6, subarray=[9]
Iterations: i=0, current=0, subarray=[1,2,3]; i=1, current=1, subarray=[2,3]; i=2, current=2, subarray=[3]
Iterations: i=0, current=100, subarray=[200,300,400]; i=1, current=200, subarray=[300,400]; i=2, current=300, subarray=[400]
Iterations: i=0, current=-1, subarray=[0,1]; i=1, current=0, subarray=[1]
Iterations: i=0, current=2, subarray=[4,6,8,10]; i=1, current=4, subarray=[6,8,10]; i=2, current=6, subarray=[8,10]; i=3, current=8, subarray=[10]
Iterations: i=0, current=1, subarray=[1,1]; i=1, current=1, subarray=[1]
Iterations: i=0, current=5, subarray=[10,15,20,25]; i=1, current=10, subarray=[15,20,25]; i=2, current=15, subarray=[20,25]; i=3, current=20, subarray=[25]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Slicing
ladders.desc: Access elements and create subarrays using indices in a list.
ladders.explanation: This level covers fundamental operations on arrays: accessing elements by index and extracting subarrays (slices). The intuition is that arrays are ordered collections, and we can refer to specific positions or ranges to manipulate data. Indexing retrieves a single element, while slicing creates a new list from a contiguous range. Step-by-step: 1. Indexing: Use arr[i] to get element at position i (0-based). Ensure i is within 0 to len(arr)-1. 2. Slicing: Use arr[start:end] to get elements from index start to end-1. If start or end omitted, they default to 0 and len(arr). Negative indices count from the end. Time: O(1) for indexing, O(k) for slicing where k is slice size. Space: O(k) for slicing as it creates a new list. Connection to L3: These operations are used in L3 to get current element and subarray. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in slicing (remember end is exclusive), indexing out of bounds, and misunderstanding negative indices. Real code pattern: arr = [1,2,3,4]; element = arr[0]; # 1; sub = arr[1:3]; # [2,3]; full = arr[:]; # copy.

examples.trace: Step 1: Indexing: arr[2] returns element at position 2: 30. Step 2: Slicing: arr[1:4] returns elements from index 1 to 3: [20,30,40].
Step 1: Indexing: arr[0] returns 5. Step 2: Slicing: arr[0:2] returns elements from index 0 to 1: [5,6].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Sums
ladders.desc: Basic concepts: what is an array, and how to compute sums of elements.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces foundational concepts for beginners: arrays and addition. An array is an ordered collection of elements, like a list of numbers. Each element has a position called an index, starting from 0. Summing involves adding two numbers together. The intuition is that to find a pair with a given sum, we need to check combinations of elements and see if their addition equals the target. Step-by-step: 1. Array: Define arr = [a0, a1, ..., an-1]. Access elements via indices. 2. Sum: For two numbers x and y, sum = x + y. 3. Pair-finding: Check if there exist indices i and j such that arr[i] + arr[j] = target. Time: O(1) for basic operations. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This foundation leads to using array operations in L4. Common mistakes include confusing indices with values, not understanding zero-based indexing, and incorrect sum calculations. Real code pattern: arr = [1,2,3]; x = arr[0]; y = arr[1]; sum = x + y; # 3.

examples.input: arr = [5, 10, 15], check if any pair sums to 20
examples.trace: Step 1: List pairs: (5,10) sum=15, (5,15) sum=20, (10,15) sum=25. Step 2: Found pair (5,15) with sum 20.

examples.input: arr = [1, 2], check if any pair sums to 4
examples.trace: Step 1: Only pair: (1,2) sum=3. Step 2: 3 != 4, so no pair sums to 4.
